reasoning: C is strongest: it runs an explicit arithmetic disconfirming test (one-time ~15-17% capacity bump vs ~4% population over two years), states a calibrated 85% confidence, names the unfalsifiability flaw ('too easy to vary'), invokes Meadows' shifting-the-burden trap, and adds a concrete honesty mechanism (publish VMT and ridership alongside speed). B matches C on systems framing and Meadows' leverage hierarchy and adds the selective-skeptic check, but its population test is qualitative rather than numerically worked. D is well-sourced (Duranton-Turner elasticity ~1.0, named pricing schemes) with a clean implementation list, but offers the least disconfirmation and no explicit uncertainty quantification. A is sound and actionable but the shallowest: it asserts the mechanism without a numeric test, gives a looser elasticity range, and surfaces fewer cognitive traps.
